alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model captures different types of information of genome composition and can be used as a recommendation system to further investigate sequences labeled as 'unknown' by conventional alignment methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Exploring these highly-divergent viruses can enhance our knowledge of infectious causes of diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Exploring these highly-divergent viruses can enhance our knowledge of infectious causes of diseases.
alignments.rationale: The high-level implication about studying divergent viruses is preserved identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ProteinPrompt achieves an accuracy rate of 0.88 and an area under curve of 0.95 using a random forest method on a rigorously filtered test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ProteinPrompt achieves an accuracy rate of 0.88 and an area under the curve of 0.95 using a random forest algorithm on a rigorously filtered test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numbers, method (random forest/algorithm), dataset description and certainty are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The graph neural network implementation of ProteinPrompt achieved an accuracy rate of 0.86 on the same test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The graph neural network implementation of ProteinPrompt achieved an accuracy rate of 0.86 on the same test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim text, metric and dataset are identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The consensus prediction method of ProteinPrompt reached an accuracy of 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The consensus prediction method of ProteinPrompt reached an accuracy of 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The consensus accuracy claim is identical between the two documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ProteinPrompt allows for the scanning of the human proteome for potential binding partners of an input sequence within minutes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ProteinPrompt allows for the scanning of the human proteome for potential binding partners of an input sequence within minutes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim about proteome scanning is word-for-word the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural networks (DNNs) outperform support vector machines (SVMs) and random forests (RFs) when trained on frequency matrix chaos game representation (FCGR) encoded protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural networks (DNNs) outperform support vector machines (SVMs) and random forests (RFs) when trained on frequency matrix chaos game representation (FCGR) encoded protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The methodological comparison and conclusion are presented identically in the preprint and the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All applied machine learning techniques (RF, SVM, and DNN) show promising results compared to state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All applied machine learning techniques (RF, SVM, and DNN) show promising results compared to state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about all methods showing promising results is retained verbatim; minor editorial differences could exist but the meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frequency matrix chaos game representation (FCGR) is a promising new encoding method for protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Frequency matrix chaos game representation (FCGR) is a promising new encoding method for protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about FCGR being a promising encoding method is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The original chaos game representation (CGR) has been modified to work for protein sequences, resulting in n-flakes representation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not contain a matching claim about modifying CGR to an n-flakes representation, so this contribution appears to have been removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) algorithms have been shown to outperform Transfer Learning (TL) based methods for a variety of tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state a general superiority of MIL over TL; the preprint claim about MIL outperforming TL is absent in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HistoMIL is a Python package designed to simplify the implementation, training, and inference process of MIL-based algorithms for computational pathologists and biomedical researchers. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HistoMIL is a Python package designed to streamline the implementation, training, and inference process of MIL-based algorithms for computational pathologists and biomedical researchers. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The package description is preserved (simplify -> streamline is editorial), so the contribution claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HistoMIL demonstrates AUROC performances of up to 85% when building predictive models for 2,487 cancer hallmark genes on breast cancer histology slides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HistoMIL achieves AUROC performances of up to 85% when building predictive models for 2,487 cancer hallmark genes on breast cancer histology slides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported AUROC performance and task are the same in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cell proliferation processes were most easily detected, shedding light on the opportunities but also limitations of applying deep learning for gene expression detection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific conclusion about cell proliferation being most easily detected is not present in the publication, indicating it was dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FASTCAR can predict the pair-wise identity score in linear time and space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identity can predict pairwise identity scores in linear time and space. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The functionality (linear time/space prediction of pairwise identity) is stated identically; only the tool name differs (FASTCAR vs Identity).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FASTCAR provided the best compromise between sensitivity and precision on two large-scale sequence databases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identity provided the best compromise between sensitivity and precision while being faster than BLAST, Mash, MUMmer4, and USEARCH by 2-80 times. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the performance claim by adding comparisons to additional tools and a wider reported speedup range, altering the original performance framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FASTCAR is faster than BLAST by 40% and faster than USEARCH by 6–10 times. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identity provided the best compromise between sensitivity and precision while being faster than BLAST, Mash, MUMmer4, and USEARCH by 2-80 times. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a broader set of speed comparisons and a wider (2–80×) speed range, which revises the specific speedup figures given in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FASTCAR is capable of producing the pair-wise identity scores of long DNA sequences, which cannot be accomplished by any alignment-based tool. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identity is capable of producing pairwise identity scores of millions-of-nucleotides-long bacterial genomes, a task that cannot be accomplished by any global-alignment-based tool. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication constrains the claim to millions-length bacterial genomes and uses 'global-alignment-based' terminology, narrowing and clarifying the original broader statement about long DNA sequences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Folding-upon-binding predominantly occurs via two distinct encounter complexes differentiated by binding orientation, helical content, and conformational heterogeneity of N<sub>TAIL</sub>. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Folding-upon-binding predominantly occurs via two distinct encounter complexes differentiated by binding orientation, helical content, and conformational heterogeneity of N<sub>TAIL</sub>. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The description of two distinct encounter complexes and their differentiating features is presented identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is no evidence for the existence of canonical conformational selection or induced fit binding pathways. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is no evidence for the existence of canonical conformational selection pathways in the binding process. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the 'no evidence' conclusion for conformational selection but drops the blanket statement about induced fit (in fact, the publication asserts an induced fit mechanism elsewhere), so the original combined claim is revised and split.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Four kinetically separated native-like bound states interconvert on time scales of eighty to five hundred nanoseconds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four kinetically separated native-like bound states interconvert on timescales of eighty to five hundred nanoseconds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The kinetic separation and timescale statement is the same in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The bound states share a core set of native intermolecular contacts and stable N<sub>TAIL</sub> helices, differentiated by a sequential formation of native and non-native contacts and additional helical turns. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analyses provide an atomic resolution structural description of intermediate states in a folding-upon-binding pathway. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a more general atomic-resolution description that encompasses the preprint's detailed observations about shared contacts and helices but omits some of the specific mechanistic text, so the claim is clarified and somewhat generalized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analyses provide an atomic resolution structural description of intermediate states in a folding-upon-binding pathway and elucidate the nature of the kinetic barriers between metastable states. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analyses provide an atomic resolution structural description of intermediate states in a folding-upon-binding pathway. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the atomic-resolution description but omits the explicit statement about elucidating kinetic barriers, narrowing the scope of the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The F1 scores of the best models on the test set are 82.0% for random allocation, 81.6% for blinded assessment of outcome, 82.6% for conflict of interests, 91.4% for compliance with animal welfare regulations, and 46.6% for reporting animals excluded from analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The F1 scores of the best models on the test set are 82.0% for random allocation, 81.6% for blinded assessment of outcome, 82.6% for conflict of interests, 91.4% for compliance with animal welfare regulations, and 46.6% for reporting animals excluded from analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The texts and reported metrics are identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our models significantly outperform regular expressions for four risk of bias items. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our models significantly outperform regular expressions for four risk of bias items. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about outperforming regular expressions is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural models achieve good performance for random allocation, blinded assessment of outcome, conflict of interests, and animal exclusions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural models achieve good performance for random allocation, blinded assessment of outcome, conflict of interests, and animal exclusions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same; only the label (conclusion vs result) differs, so the substantive claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BERT model with sentence extraction strategy works better for compliance with animal welfare regulations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific assertion that a BERT sentence-extraction strategy was best does not appear in the publication, which instead reports different best-performing architectures.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural networks are the overall best models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit claim that CNNs are overall best, which was not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The tool is publicly available which may contribute to the future monitoring of risk of bias reporting for research improvement activities. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a statement about public availability and intended contribution that was not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Generative modeling, particularly through diffusion models, has the potential to synthesize medical images effectively. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Generative modeling through diffusion models has unlocked remarkable capabilities in synthesizing photorealistic images for medical imaging. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication upgrades the language from potential to having 'unlocked remarkable capabilities', strengthening the claim (amplified confidence).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of diffusion models in generating medical images successfully captures attributes specific to oncology within imaging modalities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of diffusion models effectively captures oncology-specific attributes across different imaging modalities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core claim is the same, but the publication expresses it with somewhat lower certainty (high->medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generation of medical imaging data using diffusion models can help preserve the anonymity of medical images and reduce the likelihood of patient re-identification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study successfully trained stable diffusion models to generate diverse medical images while preserving patient anonymity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms and reports successful preservation of anonymity (stronger/result-level claim), increasing confidence relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This research establishes a framework that harnesses the power of artificial intelligence for the generation of cancer medical imagery. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Generative modeling through diffusion models has unlocked remarkable capabilities in synthesizing photorealistic images for medical imaging. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution-level claim in the preprint about a framework for AI-generated cancer imagery aligns with the publication's high-level contribution statement; content is retained.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Fréchet inception distance metric was used to evaluate the quality of synthesized images, demonstrating high fidelity between synthesized and real images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The use of the FID metric and quantitative fidelity result is presented in the publication but was not stated as a claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning can significantly improve classification performance in the context of meta-estimators for uncategorised nsSNVs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning can significantly improve both prediction accuracy and coverage in the classification of phenotypical effects of uncategorised nsSNVs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint claim about improved classification performance aligns with the publication's explicit statement about improved accuracy and coverage; the substantive claim is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ability of neural networks to discover intricate structures in highly heterogeneous datasets can aid a meta-estimator. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ability of neural networks to discover intricate structures in highly heterogeneous datasets can aid a meta-estimator. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Even relatively simple modern neural networks can significantly improve both prediction accuracy and coverage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning can significantly improve both prediction accuracy and coverage in the classification of phenotypical effects of uncategorised nsSNVs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This preprint result maps directly onto the publication's statement about improvements in accuracy and coverage.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shortage of high-quality reference data and bias towards intensively studied in vitro models necessitates improved generalisation ability for better classification accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The specific limitation about shortage/bias in reference data is not represented among the publication claims provided, so it appears not carried forward as a top-level claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Overfitting was hindered by noise injection and dropout, limiting coadaptation of hidden units in the deep learning models used. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific methodological/result claim about preventing overfitting that was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The research provides open-access to the finest model developed for predicting phenotypical effects of nsSNVs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's statement about providing open-access to the model is an added contribution claim not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepNeuron demonstrates robustness and accuracy in neuron tracing using light microscopy images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepNeuron demonstrates robustness and accuracy in neuron tracing using light microscopy images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result claim is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepNeuron uses deep learning networks to learn features and rules from data for tracing neuron morphology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepNeuron uses deep learning networks to learn features and rules from data for tracing neuron morphology. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepNeuron provides a family of modules to solve basic yet challenging problems in neuron tracing. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepNeuron provides a family of modules to solve basic yet challenging problems in neuron tracing. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The modular contribution claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepNeuron can detect neuron signals under different image conditions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepNeuron can detect neuron signals under different image conditions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The detection-under-varying-conditions claim is identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Transformer Graph Variational Autoencoder (TGVAE) captures complex structural relationships within molecules more effectively than traditional SMILES representations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The transformer graph variational autoencoder (TGVAE) effectively captures complex structural relationships within molecules using molecular graphs as input data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains that TGVAE captures complex structural relationships but drops the explicit comparative claim to SMILES, narrowing and tempering the original comparative assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TGVAE combines a Transformer, Graph Neural Network (GNN), and Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to enhance molecular generation capabilities. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The transformer graph variational autoencoder (TGVAE) effectively captures complex structural relationships within molecules using molecular graphs as input data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication names and claims the TGVAE as a contribution; although the component-level description is implicit, the contribution claim is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TGVAE outperforms existing approaches by generating a larger collection of diverse molecules and discovering previously unexplored structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TGVAE outperforms existing approaches by generating a larger collection of diverse molecules. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the outperforming/diversity claim but omits the explicit claim about discovering previously unexplored structures, narrowing the scope of the original result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The advancements made by TGVAE set a new level for the use of AI in molecular generation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The advancement of TGVAE sets a new level for the use of AI in molecular generation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The high-level concluding statement is essentially the same in both versions.

alignments.pub_claim_text: TGVAE addresses common issues like over-smoothing in training GNNs and posterior collapse in VAEs to ensure robust training. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific training/technical-robustness claim (addressing over-smoothing/posterior collapse) that was not present as a top-level claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The small intestine comprises five distinct domains of nutrient absorption, rather than the traditionally recognized three segments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The small intestine comprises five domains of nutrient absorption that respond distinctly to dietary changes.
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert five absorption domains; the publication adds that these domains respond differently to diet, which clarifies and expands the original claim without changing its certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spatially restricted expression programs in nutrient-absorbing enterocytes originate from intestinal stem cells in three regional populations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Three regional stem cell populations were identified in the small intestine.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the identification of three regional stem cell populations described in the preprint; the higher stated certainty in the paper makes this an amplification of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The functions of Ppar-ẟ and Cdx1 are established in patterning lipid metabolism in the distal domains of the small intestine.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A predictive model of transcriptional control of domain identity was generated and validated for Ppar-δ and Cdx1.
alignments.rationale: Both statements concern Ppar-δ and Cdx1 controlling domain identity/lipid patterns; the publication frames this as a validated predictive model, which clarifies the mechanistic evidence without changing the overall certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Molecular domain identity can be detected using machine learning, providing a systematic method to identify specific intestinal regions in mice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Molecular domain identity in the small intestine can be detected using machine learning.
alignments.rationale: The core claim that ML can detect domain identity is the same; the publication states it with slightly lower reported certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our methodology significantly reduces the labor of the anatomist while improving the consistency of the annotation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our methodology significantly reduces the labor of the anatomist while improving the consistency of the annotation.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological conclusion verbatim with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method can reduce the time to annotate by as much as ten-fold without significantly increasing the rate of errors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method can reduce the time to annotate by as much as ten-fold without significantly increasing the rate of errors.
alignments.rationale: The claim about ten-fold time reduction and error rates is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method achieves significant reduction in labor while achieving an accuracy that is similar to the level of agreement between different anatomists.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method achieves significant reduction in labor while achieving an accuracy that is similar to the level of agreement between different anatomists.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance comparison to inter-anatomist agreement with no change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generalization performance of parcelwise connectivity profiles trained on single dataset samples is dependent on the specific test samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generalization performance of parcelwise classifiers (pwCs) trained on single dataset samples is dependent on the specific test samples.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same dependency of generalization on test sample identity with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Certain datasets seem to 'match' such that classifiers trained on a sample from one dataset achieve high accuracy when tested on the respective other one.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Certain datasets seem to 'match' in the sense that classifiers trained on a sample from one dataset achieved a high accuracy when tested on the respected other one and vice versa.
alignments.rationale: Both texts describe reciprocal dataset 'matching' yielding high cross-dataset accuracy; the publication language is slightly rephrased but unchanged in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The parcelwise connectivity model trained on the compound sample demonstrated the overall highest generalization performance for all test samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pwCs trained on the compound samples demonstrated overall highest generalization performance for all test samples, including one derived from a dataset not included in building the training samples.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that the compound-trained model generalized even to a held-out dataset, clarifying and slightly broadening the original claim about overall best performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A big and heterogeneous training sample comprising data from multiple datasets is best suited to achieve generalizable results.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both a large sample size and a heterogeneous data composition of a training sample have a central role in achieving generalizable results.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion about the importance of size and heterogeneity for generalizability with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel systems-based methylation clocks developed in this study capture aging in distinct physiological systems when assessed in blood.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed systems-based DNA methylation clocks measure aging in 11 distinct physiological systems using data from a single blood draw.
alignments.rationale: Both claims state the clocks measure system-specific aging from blood; the publication specifies '11 systems' and 'single blood draw', which clarifies and narrows the earlier, less specific wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The panel of 11 system-specific scores predicts a wide variety of outcomes, aging phenotypes, and conditions specific to their respective systems.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The system-specific scores derived from integrating machine learning with clinical biomarkers outperformed existing global clocks in predicting relevant diseases and aging phenotypes.
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts to a comparative performance claim (outperforming global clocks) and links scores to ML and biomarkers; this revises and narrows the original predictive claim while increasing stated impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The composite Systems Age clock is predictive of aging across physiological systems in an unbiased manner.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework developed enables a more granular and clinically relevant assessment of biological aging.
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes an unbiased composite clock; the publication emphasizes the framework's clinical granularity—related but not fully overlapping, so the mapping is partial and less certain.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system scores cluster individuals into unique aging subtypes with different patterns of age-related disease and decline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clustering individuals based on the system-specific scores revealed distinct biological aging subtypes associated with unique patterns of health decline and disease risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the clustering result with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This biological systems-based epigenetic framework may inform the development of more personalized clinical approaches for improving age-related quality of life.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework developed enables a more granular and clinically relevant assessment of biological aging.
alignments.rationale: Both claims link the framework to clinical utility; the publication states this more strongly (higher certainty) and more concretely as enabling granular, clinically relevant assessment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony predicted the probability of groups reaching the correct consensus decision with greater than 70% cross-validation accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony predicted the probability of groups reaching the correct consensus decision with over 70% cross-validation accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative predictive result with identical meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony was significantly more predictive of correct decision-making than the duration of discussions, subjective assessment of team function, or baseline heart rates alone.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony was significantly more predictive of correct decision-making than the duration of discussions, subjective assessment of team function, or baseline heart rates alone.
alignments.rationale: The comparative predictive finding is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony during group discussion provides a biomarker of interpersonal engagement that facilitates adaptive learning and effective information sharing during collective decision-making.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heart rate synchrony during group discussion provides a biomarker of interpersonal engagement that facilitates adaptive learning and effective information sharing during collective decision-making.
alignments.rationale: The interpretive conclusion about HR synchrony as a biomarker of engagement and its functional role is identical in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated segmentation methods produce exceptionally accurate reconstructions of cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated segmentation methods produce exceptionally accurate reconstructions of cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim verbatim with the same certainty and scope, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Post-hoc proofreading is still required to generate large connectomes free of merge and split errors. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Post hoc proofreading is still required to generate large connectomes that are free of merge and split errors. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation with only minor punctuation differences and identical meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The software package 'NEURD' decomposes meshed neurons into compact and extensively-annotated graph representations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural Decomposition (NEURD) is a software package that decomposes meshed neurons into compact and extensively annotated graph representations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same contribution statement with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NEURD automates a variety of tasks such as automated proofreading of merge errors, cell classification, and spine detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The feature-rich graphs produced by NEURD automate a variety of tasks such as automated proofreading of merge errors and cell classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains automation of proofreading and classification but omits spine detection, narrowing the set of claimed automated tasks while keeping the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The features of NEURD enable many downstream analyses of neural morphology and connectivity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NEURD enables many downstream analyses of neural morphology and connectivity, making complex datasets more accessible to neuroscience researchers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands and clarifies the downstream impact (adds accessibility framing) and raises the stated certainty from medium to high, amplifying confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 RNA genome and all sgRNAs are enriched in the host mitochondrial matrix and nucleolus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 RNA genome and sgRNAs are predicted to be enriched toward the host mitochondrial matrix and nucleolus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication hedges the original categorical statement into a predictive interpretation, maintaining the same loci but tempering assertiveness about observed enrichment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 5' and 3' viral untranslated regions possess the strongest and most distinct localization signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 5' and 3' viral untranslated regions contain the strongest, most distinct localization signals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result with equivalent wording and certainty; no substantive change is present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mitochondrial localization signal is related to the formation of double-membrane vesicles, which is a critical stage in the coronavirus life cycle. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mitochondrial residency signal is interpreted as an indicator of intracellular RNA trafficking with respect to double-membrane vesicles. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the original causal linkage into an interpretation about RNA trafficking relative to double-membrane vesicles, narrowing the mechanistic claim while keeping similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational analysis serves as a hypothesis generation tool to suggest models for SARS-CoV-2 biology. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computational analysis serves as a hypothesis generation tool to suggest models for SARS-CoV-2 biology. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same statement about the computational analysis as a hypothesis-generation tool with unchanged scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning and deep learning with longitudinal features improved the 10-year CVD risk prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating longitudinal features leads to better cardiovascular disease event prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same core result—that longitudinal features improve CVD event prediction—using slightly broader wording but preserving meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating genetic features further enhanced 10-year CVD prediction performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining genetic features through a late-fusion approach can further improve cardiovascular disease prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the method (late-fusion) for combining genetic features, clarifying how genetic information contributes while preserving the overall claim that genetics can improve prediction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The late fusion approach achieved a significant improvement for the prediction performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining genetic features through a late-fusion approach can further improve cardiovascular disease prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the late-fusion result as a method that can improve prediction (less explicit about the magnitude 'significant improvement'), so the claim is clarified with a milder framing of effect size.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LSTM, CNN and GBT with temporal features achieved AUROC values of 0.789, 0.790, and 0.791, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report these model-specific AUROC numbers or the explicit comparison, so this specific numerical result is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning approach can directly estimate species richness without the need to estimate individual species ranges. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning approach can directly estimate species richness without the need to estimate individual species ranges. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same methodological contribution verbatim with equal certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network model predicts species richness based on spatially associated variables, including climatic and geographic predictors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The neural network model predicts species richness based on spatially associated variables, including climatic and geographic predictors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is repeated in the publication with the same predictors and certainty, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach produces independently verifiable maps of alpha, beta, and gamma plant diversity at high spatial resolutions for Australia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach produces independently verifiable maps of alpha, beta, and gamma plant diversity at high spatial resolutions for Australia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this specific result about biodiversity maps with identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning framework offers a powerful and flexible new method for estimating biodiversity patterns. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This deep learning framework represents a significant advancement toward automated biodiversity assessments. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the strong high-certainty claim as a more measured statement about advancement toward automated assessments and lowers certainty, tempering the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Exosomal miRNAs have potential applications in liquid biopsy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not restate the general conclusion about liquid biopsy applications, so this higher-level application claim is not present in the publication summary.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An ensemble method has been developed to predict and design exosomal miRNA. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A web server, EmiRPred, has been developed to assist in predicting and designing exosomal miRNAs. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a concrete implementation (a web server) of the claimed ensemble method, clarifying the contribution and broadening accessibility by providing a tool rather than only a method description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI models achieved a maximum AUC of 0.707 and an MCC of 0.268 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI models achieved a maximum AUC of 0.707 and an MCC of 0.268 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact performance metrics for the AI models on the independent dataset with identical certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ensemble method reached a maximum AUC of 0.73 and an MCC of 0.352 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ensemble method reached a maximum AUC of 0.73 and an MCC of 0.352 on an independent dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the reported ensemble performance metrics verbatim and with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A variety of important features and motifs for exosomal miRNA have been identified. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Specific nucleotides are preferred at certain positions in miRNAs associated with exosomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies particular nucleotide positional preferences (more concrete than the preprint's general identification of features), increasing specificity and reported certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different general anesthetics affect cerebral electrical activities in different ways. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Different general anesthetics affect cerebral electrical activities in different ways. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The two statements are effectively identical in wording and meaning, so the claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conventional machine learning models perform unsatisfactorily on EEG data due to low Signal to Noise Ratio (SNR). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of conventional machine learning models on EEG data is unsatisfactory due to the low Signal to Noise Ratio in the EEG signals. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation (low SNR causing unsatisfactory performance) with equivalent meaning; only the claim type label differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed Anes-MetaNet framework effectively classifies brain states under anesthetics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed Anes-MetaNet framework effectively classifies brain states under anesthetics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is reproduced verbatim and retains the same scope and confidence in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multi-stage training paradigm used in Anes-MetaNet improves performance as justified by visualizing high-level feature mapping. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experiments on the office-based anesthesia EEG dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of Anes-MetaNet by comparison of existing methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes a mechanistic improvement via multi-stage training and visualization; the publication instead highlights empirical demonstration on a specific dataset and comparison to methods, shifting and narrowing the focus and changing how the claim is justified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DepoScope is a machine learning tool that accurately identifies phage depolymerase sequences and their enzymatic domains. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DepoScope is a machine learning tool that accurately identifies depolymerase sequences and their enzymatic domains. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core result/claim about DepoScope's accurate identification of depolymerases and domains with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current methods to annotate phage depolymerases and their associated enzymatic domains are challenging due to high sequence diversity. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This limitation about high sequence diversity and annotation difficulty does not appear among the publication claims and thus is omitted in the published list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DepoScope combines sequence-level predictions with amino-acid-level predictions for accurate depolymerase detection. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach used in DepoScope combines sequence-level predictions with amino-acid-level predictions for accurate depolymerase detection. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the method combination claim verbatim; content, scope, and confidence are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DepoScope can enhance our understanding of phage-host interactions at the level of depolymerases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DepoScope can enhance our understanding of phage-host interactions at the level of depolymerases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this conclusion about biological insight with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DepoScope code and database are publicly accessible to promote further research in the community. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims list provided does not mention public code/database availability, so this contribution appears omitted from the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The rawMSA method outperforms classical methods based on position-specific scoring matrices (PSSM) when predicting secondary structure and solvent accessibility. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rawMSA methodology outperforms classical methods based on position-specific scoring matrices (PSSM) when predicting secondary structure and solvent accessibility. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is restated verbatim in the publication, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: rawMSA performs on par with the top ranked CASP12 methods in the inter-residue contact map prediction category. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: rawMSA performs on par with methods using more pre-calculated features in the inter-residue contact map prediction category in CASP12 and CASP13. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the comparison scope (includes CASP13 and methods using more pre-calculated features) and expresses the result with lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and broadened but tempered in confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: rawMSA represents a promising, more powerful approach to protein structure prediction that could replace older methods based on protein profiles. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: rawMSA represents a promising development that can pave the way for improved methods using rawMSA instead of sequence profiles to represent evolutionary information. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same forward-looking conclusion but with stronger language/certainty and emphasis on paving the way for improved methods, so the claim is clarified and the confidence amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The core idea behind rawMSA is to map amino acid sequences into an adaptively learned continuous space, allowing the whole MSA to be input into a Deep Network. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The core idea behind rawMSA is to map amino acid sequences into an adaptively learned continuous space, allowing the whole MSA to be input into a Deep Network. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The conceptual contribution is stated the same way, but the publication lists it with somewhat lower certainty, so the semantic content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning applied to echocardiography can identify local cardiac structures and estimate cardiac function. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning applied to echocardiography can identify local cardiac structures and estimate cardiac function. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this broad result identically, preserving scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The EchoNet model accurately identified the presence of pacemaker leads with an AUC of 0.89. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The EchoNet model accurately identified the presence of pacemaker leads with an AUC of 0.89 and enlarged left atrium with an AUC of 0.86. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The pacemaker-lead result is preserved, but the publication augments the claim by reporting an additional finding (enlarged left atrium), so the original claim is clarified and the overall scope broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning on echocardiography images can streamline repetitive tasks in the clinical workflow. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning on echocardiography images can streamline repetitive tasks in the clinical workflow. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion with the same framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoNet predicted systemic phenotypes such as age, sex, weight, and height with varying degrees of accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoNet predicts systemic phenotypes such as age, sex, weight, and height that are challenging for human evaluation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern predicting systemic phenotypes; the publication frames these predictions as especially challenging for humans and reports higher certainty, so the claim is clarified and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Interpretation analysis validates that EchoNet shows appropriate attention to key cardiac structures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Interpretation analysis validates that EchoNet shows appropriate attention to key cardiac structures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The interpretation/attention claim is reproduced verbatim with the same certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Category-selective tuning naturally emerges for faces, bodies, scenes, and words in models trained with contrastive self-supervised objectives. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Category-selective tuning naturally emerges for faces, bodies, scenes, and words in models trained with contrastive self-supervised objectives. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lesions of model units lead to selective, dissociable recognition deficits, highlighting their distinct functional roles in information processing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lesions of model units lead to selective, dissociable recognition deficits, highlighting their distinct functional roles in information processing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The lesion-result claim is reproduced without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pre-identified units can predict neural responses in all corresponding face-, scene-, body-, and word-selective regions of the human visual cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pre-identified units can predict neural responses in all corresponding face-, scene-, body-, and word-selective regions of the human visual cortex. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The predictive mapping claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The success of the single model indicates that brain-like functional specialization can emerge without category-specific learning pressures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The success of this single model indicates that brain-like functional specialization can emerge without category-specific learning pressures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about emergence of specialization is preserved unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Contrastive coding provides a unifying account of object category emergence and representation in the human brain. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Contrastive coding provides a unifying account of object category emergence and representation in the human brain. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The broader theoretical claim about contrastive coding is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A graph neural network based method can learn an effective master equation for time-dependent marginal probability distributions of mRNA and protein numbers at sub-cellular resolution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A graph neural network based approach can predict the dynamics of reaction-diffusion processes in larger and more complex spaces using cheap Monte Carlo simulations.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the GNN-based modeling contribution but reframes it from learning a master equation for mRNA/protein marginals to predicting general reaction–diffusion dynamics in larger/complex spaces, broadening the application and changing the technical framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The numerical solution of the master equation leads to accurate results in a small fraction of the computation time compared to standard simulation methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method leads to accurate results in a small fraction of the computation time compared to standard stochastic simulation methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance claim (accuracy and much lower computation time versus standard stochastic simulation), with only minor wording changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictions of the method can be extrapolated to unseen spatial organization and regions of parameter space.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A graph neural network based approach can predict the dynamics of reaction-diffusion processes in larger and more complex spaces using cheap Monte Carlo simulations.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes generalization to larger/complex spatial domains (consistent with extrapolating to unseen spatial organization) but does not explicitly claim extrapolation across parameter space, so the claim is clarified toward spatial generalization and stated with higher confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The scalability and accuracy of the method suggest it is a promising approach for whole cell modeling.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The scalability and accuracy of the method suggest it is a promising approach for studying reaction-diffusion processes in complex spatial domains.
alignments.rationale: Both versions draw the same high-level conclusion about promise, but the publication shifts the target from whole-cell modeling to studying reaction–diffusion processes in complex spatial domains, narrowing the intended application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep graph neural network, ProteinSolver, can solve the protein design problem by phrasing it as a constraint satisfaction problem.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ProteinSolver can precisely design sequences that fold into a predetermined shape by phrasing this challenge as a constraint satisfaction problem.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same primary claim that ProteinSolver formulates protein design as a constraint satisfaction problem and produces sequences folding to predetermined shapes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The network is trained on millions of real protein sequences corresponding to thousands of protein structures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We trained ProteinSolver on over 70,000,000 real protein sequences corresponding to over 80,000 structures.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides much more precise and substantially larger training-data numbers than the preprint, clarifying and amplifying the original training-data claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method rapidly designs novel protein sequences that adopt predetermined structures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method rapidly designs new protein sequences and benchmarks them in silico using energy-based scores, molecular dynamics, and structure prediction methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the rapid design claim and adds explicit in silico benchmarking methods, strengthening/clarifying the evidence provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The designed proteins undergo a variety of in silico and in vitro validations suggesting they adopt the predetermined structures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We use ProteinSolver to generate sequences that match the structure of serum albumin and validate it in vitro using circular dichroism.
alignments.rationale: The preprint claimed a variety of in silico and in vitro validations; the publication reports a specific in vitro validation (serum albumin via CD), narrowing the claim to a concrete example while stating it with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study enabled a systemic identification and holistic classification of Cdc42 homeostatic modulators (HMs).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Mant-GTP fluorophore-based Cdc42-GEF screening uncovered a near holistic spectrum of HMs for Cdc42.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe a broad/holistic identification of Cdc42 HMs from screening; the publication frames it as a 'near holistic spectrum' uncovered by a specific assay, clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Five functionally distinct classes of Cdc42 HMs were uncovered, including partial competitive agonists, hormetic agonists, bona fide inhibitors, bona fide activators, and ligand-enhanced agonists.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Mant-GTP fluorophore-based Cdc42-GEF screening uncovered a near holistic spectrum of HMs for Cdc42.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports uncovering a near-holistic spectrum of HMs but does not enumerate the five distinct functional classes in the same explicit way; the core result is retained though somewhat less granular in wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Novel HMs demonstrated significant biological functionality in bradykinin-Cdc42 activation of actin remodeling and modified Alzheimer's disease-like behavior in a mouse model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model HMs elicited striking biological functionalities in modulating bradykinin activation of Cdc42 signaling as well as actin remodeling.
alignments.rationale: The publication substantiates the bradykinin–Cdc42 and actin-remodeling functional effects but omits mention of modified Alzheimer's-like mouse behavior, narrowing the claimed in vivo/behavioral result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlighted the power of computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) to predict HM classes that can mimic pharmacological functionality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study highlights a model path to actively capture different classes of HM to broaden the therapeutic landscape.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the conclusion toward a translational/model path to capture HM classes for therapeutics; this clarifies and broadens the practical implications relative to the preprint's emphasis on CADD prediction power.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Molecular re-docking of HM model compounds led to the concept of preferential binding pocket order (PBPO) for Cdc42-ITSN interactions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Molecular structural modeling analyses led to the concept of preferential binding pocket order (PBPO) for profiling HMs that target Cdc42.
alignments.rationale: Both versions report that molecular modeling produced the PBPO concept; the publication uses slightly broader phrasing but retains the same contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study compiles a set of 37 machine learning methods for in vivo RBP-RNA interaction prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study compiles a set of 37 machine learning methods for in vivo RBP-RNA interaction prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the compilation claim verbatim; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A subset of 11 representative methods was systematically benchmarked across hundreds of CLIP-seq datasets and RBPs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A subset of 11 representative methods is systematically benchmarked across hundreds of CLIP-seq datasets and RBPs.
alignments.rationale: The benchmarking claim is preserved in the publication with essentially identical scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evaluation of methods was conducted using homogenized sample pre-processing and two negative-class sample generation strategies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study evaluates methods in terms of predictive performance and assesses the impact of neural network architectures and input modalities on model performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts emphasis from specific preprocessing and negative-sampling protocols to broader evaluation axes (performance, architectures, modalities); the evaluation claim is preserved but reframed to include additional comparative factors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study aims to enable researchers to choose the optimal prediction method for their tasks.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the user-focused claim about enabling researchers to choose optimal methods; instead it emphasizes developer-focused outcomes, so the original researcher-choice claim is effectively omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of a standardized framework for method evaluation will aid developers in creating novel, high-performing methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The introduction of a standardized framework for method evaluation will aid method developers in developing novel, high-performing methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this conclusion about the standardized framework aiding method developers with equivalent meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed gmcNet generates more robust features than single-level expression or topological overlap measure.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CEPR generates more robust features than single-level expression or topological overlap measure.
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts the same comparative performance but uses the term 'CEPR' where the preprint named 'gmcNet'; the core performance claim is retained though the naming/placement is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: gmcNet achieved the best performance in terms of modularity (0.261) and the differentially expressed signal (27.739) compared with other clustering methods tested.
alignments.pub_claim_text: gmcNet achieved the best performance in terms of modularity (0.261) and differentially expressed signal (27.739) compared with other clustering methods tested.
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics and comparative claim are carried over exactly into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: gmcNet detected interesting biological functionalities for carcass weight, backfat thickness, intramuscular fat, and beef tenderness of Korean native cattle.
alignments.pub_claim_text: gmcNet detected interesting biological functionalities for carcass weight, backfat thickness, intramuscular fat, and beef tenderness of Korean native cattle.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the specific biological findings reported in the preprint without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed gmcNet works similarly to the message-passing operation of graph neural networks.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate the mechanistic analogy to GNN message-passing present in the preprint, so this claim appears to have been dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research will focus on adding noise filtering and optimal k search to gmcNet to enhance its utility as a module clustering algorithm in WGCNA.
alignments.rationale: The specific future-work items listed in the preprint are not present in the publication's claims or conclusions, indicating these planned extensions were removed from the final text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GraNA accurately predicted the functional relatedness of proteins and robustly transferred functional annotations across species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GraNA accurately predicted the functional relatedness of proteins and robustly transferred functional annotations across species. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result claim with equivalent wording and certainty, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GraNA outperformed a number of existing network alignment methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GraNA outperformed a number of existing network alignment methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is verbatim between preprint and publication, so it is unchanged in meaning, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GraNA successfully discovered functionally replaceable human-yeast protein pairs that were documented in previous studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GraNA successfully discovered functionally replaceable human-yeast protein pairs that were documented in previous studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same specific result and certainty as the preprint, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GraNA integrates multi-faceted non-functional relationship data to guide the mapping of functionally related proteins across species. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GraNA integrates multi-faceted non-functional relationship data to guide the mapping of functionally related proteins across species. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is preserved verbatim with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dopaminergic axons in the medial prefrontal cortex exhibit diverse activity in response to both reward and aversive stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dopamine axons in the medial prefrontal cortex exhibit diverse activity in response to both reward and aversive stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same observation with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a strong population-level bias in dopamine axons favoring aversive stimuli over reward stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a strong population-level bias in dopamine axons favoring aversive stimuli over reward stimuli. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The population-level bias claim is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A cue activity preference develops in aversive-preferring axons as mice learn to discriminate between reward and aversive cues. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A cue activity preference develops in aversive-preferring axons as mice learn to discriminate between reward and aversive cues. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same; the publication frames it as a result rather than a conclusion but the substantive claim and certainty remain similar.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Successful discrimination of cues is associated with sharper selectivity for the aversive cue in aversive-preferring axons. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Successful discrimination of cues is associated with sharper selectivity for the aversive cue in aversive-preferring axons. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result is restated identically in the publication with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Distinct temporal stages of sleep exist in the brains of behaving flies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distinct temporal stages of sleep have been uncovered in the brains of behaving flies using machine learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds methodological detail (machine learning) to the same discovery, clarifying how stages were identified without changing the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The spectral features of rhythmic proboscis extensions during sleep differ significantly from those during wakefulness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The spectral features of sleep-related behaviors differ significantly from those associated with the same behaviors during wakefulness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same finding about spectral differences for sleep behaviors versus wakefulness with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a dissociation between behavior and the brain states wherein certain behaviors reside. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a dissociation between behavior and the brain states wherein these behaviors reside. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conceptual conclusion is preserved in the publication with essentially identical phrasing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The first multi-tissue, single-cell gene expression atlas of female and male turquoise killifish tissues has been created. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This atlas can be a resource to the community that could be leveraged to develop cell-type-specific expression in transgenic animals. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the atlas as a community resource rather than asserting it is the 'first multi-tissue' atlas; the claim is therefore softened and narrowed in scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pervasive sex-dimorphic gene expression was found across cell types, particularly in the liver. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We find pervasive sex-dimorphic gene expression across cell types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the pervasive sex-dimorphism result with equivalent emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sex-dimorphic genes are primarily involved in processes related to lipid metabolism, with observable differences in lipid storage between female and male turquoise killifish livers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sex-dimorphic genes tend to be linked to lipid metabolism, consistent with clear differences in lipid storage in female vs. male turquoise killifish livers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same association between sex-dimorphic genes and lipid metabolism and liver lipid storage differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine-learning was used to predict sex using single-cell gene expression data, identifying potential transcriptional markers for molecular sex identity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We use machine learning to predict sex using single-cell gene expression and identify potential markers for molecular sex identity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The methodological result and its interpretation are presented similarly in the publication with comparable certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The single-cell atlas can be utilized to deconvolute existing liver bulk RNA-seq data to accurately estimate cell type proportions across biological conditions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our atlas can be used to deconvolute existing bulk RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) data to obtain accurate estimates of cell type proportions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the deconvolution capability claim but states it with higher certainty, effectively amplifying confidence in this use.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A mesenchymal-like gene expression program contributes to chemotherapy resistance in neuroblastoma. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the causal claim linking the mesenchymal-like program to chemotherapy resistance despite reporting related observations, so the specific contribution claim was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A weakly expressed, mesenchymal-like program is present in some pre-treated high-risk patient tumors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A weakly expressed, mesenchymal-like program is confirmed in some pre-treated high-risk patient tumors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The observation is retained and presented with stronger certainty in the publication, so content is unchanged but confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The weak-mesenchymal-like program was maintained in patient-derived xenograft models and could be chemotherapy-induced in a genetically engineered mouse model after only 24 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This weak-mesenchymal-like program is maintained in patient-derived xenograft models and could be chemotherapy-induced in a genetically engineered mouse model after only 24 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same experimental observations with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings improve the understanding of how neuroblastoma patient tumor heterogeneity is reflected in preclinical models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings improve the understanding of how neuroblastoma patient tumor heterogeneity is reflected in preclinical models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This interpretive conclusion is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides a comprehensive integrated resource and a generalizable set of computational methodologies for the joint analysis of clinical and pre-clinical single-cell RNA-seq datasets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides a comprehensive integrated resource and a generalizable set of computational methodologies for the joint analysis of clinical and pre-clinical single-cell RNA-seq datasets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is carried forward intact into the publication with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MFmap demonstrates high accuracy in predicting cancer subtypes with a test set F1 score greater than 90%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MFmap demonstrates an accuracy (test set F1 score >90%) in subtype prediction validated in ten different cancer datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the >90% F1 result but adds validation across ten datasets, clarifying and broadening the claim by reporting broader validation.
